coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional glutathione-dependent hydrogel eye drops with enhanced drug bioavailability for glaucoma therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Jui-Yang
Luo, Li-Jyuan
Nguyen, Duc Dung
coredata.dc:description: The development of drug carriers with advanced functions to overcome the low bioavailability of conventional eye drops is critical for the effective treatment of chronic ocular diseases. This paper reports a functional strategy for developing a new hydrogel eye drop with strong mucoadhesive, tight-junction opening, and antioxidant properties, which play important roles in improving the topical treatment of glaucoma. The eye-drop carrier was rationally designed through the conjugation of gelatin with poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (GN) via the formation of amide linkages, followed by free-radical grafting with glutathione (GSH). In vitro studies revealed that the GN-GSH hydrogels had good ocular biocompatibility, high antioxidant activity, and high paracellular permeability. Interestingly, grafting an appropriate amount of GSH to the GN backbone led to a tradeoff between the bioadhesion and biodegradation properties of the hydrogel, which prolonged its retention on the conjunctival sac of a rabbit’s eye without causing any ocular discomfort/irritation. In a rabbit glaucoma model, pharmacological treatment with a single-dose topical instillation of the optimized pilocarpine-loaded GN-GSH hydrogel effectively suppressed disease progression for 14 d, whereas that employing only a GN-based formulation mitigated glaucoma development for 3 d (modest efficacy). This significant enhancement is ascribed to the improved drug bioavailability achieved via the rational exploitation of GSH, which resulted in an eightfold increase in the pilocarpine concentration in the aqueous humor of the glaucomatous eyes. These findings can be valuable for the development of multifunctional GSH-dependent hydrogel eye drops as long-acting ophthalmic formulations for the efficient management of complex and chronic ocular diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multifunctional glutathione-dependent hydrogels
Prolonged ocular retention
Enhanced paracellular delivery
Topical glaucoma formulations
Drug bioavailability improvement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the formulation of the multifunctional GN-GSH hydrogel loaded with pilocarpine and its topical use as an in situ gelling eye drop for glaucoma therapy. TJ: tight junction; PTK: protein tyrosine kinase; PTP: protein tyrosine phosphatase; GSSG: glutathione disulfide.
(a) 1H NMR and (b) FTIR spectra of GN and GN-GSH hydrogels with different GSH grafting amounts (GSH1, GSH2, and GSH3).

(a) Water content, (b) DSC thermograms, and (c) weight loss of the GN and GN-GSH hydrogels. In (a, b), the asterisks indicate statistically significant differences (*P < 0.05; n = 4) relative to the GN group. In (c), the asterisks indicate statistically significant differences (*P < 0.05; n = 5) for the mean value of the weight-loss percentage compared with the value at the previous time point. Number signs indicate statistically significant differences (## P < 0.005; ### P < 0.001) relative to the GN group (compared only within each time point).

(a) Typical photographs of the DPPH reagent after the reaction with the GN and GN-GSH hydrogels. (b) Representative fluorescent images of SIRC cells after incubation with the hydrogels for 24 h followed by 24 h of exposure to H2O2. Cells exposed to 0 (Ctrl) or 200 μM H2O2 (HP) for 24 h without pretreatment with any hydrogels were used for comparison. The scale bars represent 50 μm. (c) TEER values of SIRC cells exposed to different types of hydrogels for 24 h. (d) Adhesion force between different types of hydrogels and the cornea. In (c, d), the asterisks indicate statistically significant differences (*P < 0.05; **P < 0.005; ***P < 0.001; n = 4) relative to the GN group.
Cumulative release percentage of pilocarpine from GN and GN-GSH hydrogel carriers at 32 °C in ATS containing MMP-9 at 160 ng/mL.

(a) Slit-lamp biomicroscopic images of rabbit eyes 1 d (d1), 7 d (d7), and 14 d (d14) after topical administration of various formulations. (b) Carrier-material retention (percentage) 1 d (d1), 7 d (d7), and 14 d (d14) after topical instillation. An asterisk indicates a statistically significant difference (*P < 0.05; n = 6) for the mean value of the percentage of carrier-material retention relative to the value at the previous time point. The number signs (#) indicate statistically significant differences (# P < 0.05; ### P < 0.001) relative to the GN group (compared only within each time point group).

Changes in the IOP after topical instillation of different hydrogel samples containing pilocarpine onto the conjunctival sac of glaucomatous (GL) rabbit eyes. Ctrl: GL rabbits without receiving the hydrogel carrier and drug. The asterisks indicate statistically significant differences (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.005; n = 6) relative to the baseline IOP values. Follow-up time point: preoperation (Pre); hour (h); day (d).

(a) Representative corneal topography maps and (b) mean K value of corneal curvature of different test rabbit eyes. (c) Typical specular microscopic images of the rabbit corneal endothelium on day 14 after topical instillation of different formulations onto the glaucomatous eyes. Glaucomatous animals receiving no hydrogel and drug served as control groups (Ctrl). (d) Quantitative analysis of specular-microscopy measurements of the corneal endothelial cell density. In (b, d), the asterisks indicate statistically significant differences (*P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001; n = 6) relative to the Pre group.

(a) Microscopic images and (b) the mean fluorescence intensities of immunofluorescence staining for the occludin of the corneal epithelium from healthy (Pre), glaucomatous (GL), and treated glaucomatous (GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3) rabbit eyes. The scale bars represent 50 μm. The pink and green dashed lines indicate the average values for healthy (Pre) and glaucomatous (GL) eyes, respectively. (c) Pilocarpine concentrations in the aqueous humor of treated glaucomatous rabbit eyes after 14 d of topical administration. In (b, c), the asterisks indicate statistically significant differences (***P < 0.001; n = 6) relative to the GN group. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Representative ERG patterns of healthy (Pre), glaucomatous (GL), and treated glaucomatous rabbit eyes after topical instillation of different GN and GN-GSH formulations. (b) Quantification of the a- and b-wave amplitudes. (c) Typical cross-sectional TEM images of optic nerves from rabbit eyes at the preoperation stage (Pre group), those afflicted with glaucoma (GL group), and those 14 d after the single topical administration (GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3 groups). The axons of the optic-nerve specimens appeared as hollow vesicles. The scale bars represent 1 μm. (d) Quantification of the axon density. In (b, d), the asterisks indicate statistically significant differences (**P < 0.005; ***P < 0.001; n = 6) relative to the Pre group.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional glutathione-dependent hydrogel eye drops with enhanced drug bioavailability for glaucoma therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of drug carriers with advanced functions to overcome the low bioavailability of conventional eye drops is critical for the effective treatment of chronic ocular diseases. This paper reports a functional strategy for developing a new hydrogel eye drop with strong mucoadhesive, tight-junction opening, and antioxidant properties, which play important roles in improving the topical treatment of glaucoma. The eye-drop carrier was rationally designed through the conjugation of gelatin with poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (GN) via the formation of amide linkages, followed by free-radical grafting with glutathione (GSH). In vitro studies revealed that the GN-GSH hydrogels had good ocular biocompatibility, high antioxidant activity, and high paracellular permeability. Interestingly, grafting an appropriate amount of GSH to the GN backbone led to a tradeoff between the bioadhesion and biodegradation properties of the hydrogel, which prolonged its retention on the conjunctival sac of a rabbit’s eye without causing any ocular discomfort/irritation. In a rabbit glaucoma model, pharmacological treatment with a single-dose topical instillation of the optimized pilocarpine-loaded GN-GSH hydrogel effectively suppressed disease progression for 14 d, whereas that employing only a GN-based formulation mitigated glaucoma development for 3 d (modest efficacy). This significant enhancement is ascribed to the improved drug bioavailability achieved via the rational exploitation of GSH, which resulted in an eightfold increase in the pilocarpine concentration in the aqueous humor of the glaucomatous eyes. These findings can be valuable for the development of multifunctional GSH-dependent hydrogel eye drops as long-acting ophthalmic formulations for the efficient management of complex and chronic ocular diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Glaucoma is an ophthalmic disorder resulting from an elevated intraocular pressure (IOP) and is associated with the progressive loss of vision [1,2]. In the period of 2012–2018, the costs associated with glaucoma treatment were estimated at £2845 per patient in the UK [3]. Considering that half of glaucomatous patients are unaware that they have this disorder and the prevalence of glaucoma is rapidly increasing (~112 million people will be affected by 2040), the true societal cost may be significantly higher [4–6]. Owing to their noninvasive nature and ease of use, topical eye drops constitute the first-line pharmacological treatment for this ocular disease. However, because glaucoma is a chronic disease that requires long-term treatment, the need for frequent instillations on the diseased eyes is a significant constraint on this administration route [7]. Moreover, high doses or frequent interventions significantly increase the risk of adverse effects [8]. The rapid clearance action caused by tear dilution and the lacrimal drainage system make a high dosing frequency necessary for eye-drop medications. Specifically, <5% of administered drug molecules can target the lesion sites located in the inner segments of the eye [9]. Therefore, it is imperative to develop an effective method for allowing eye drops to overcome the ocular barriers and thereby reduce the instillation frequency of eye drops for the efficient management of glaucoma.

Hydrogels are three-dimensional polymers with a high water-absorption capability, structural stability in aqueous media, excellent biocompatibility, and a resemblance to living tissues, which make them promising drug carriers for improving the low bioavailability of eye drops [10]. Among the various hydrogel-based drug-delivery systems developed in recent years, thermoresponsive hydrogels have attracted considerable attention because of their fascinating properties, which allow control of their physical state [11]. The most widely investigated thermosensitive hydrogel is poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAAm), which exhibits a reversible gelling behavior in aqueous media, with a gel point close to the physiological temperature [12]. However, PNIPAAm hydrogels have poor biodegradation [13] and limited applicability for drug release, possibly because of their rapid on–off switching response to thermal stimuli [14,15], which impede their use in ophthalmic applications. Several strategies have been reported for exploiting the synergistic interactions between PNIPAAm and biodegradable materials such as gelatin to sustain drug-release profiles without losing the thermosensitive characteristics [16–18]. Nevertheless, ophthalmic formulations based on thermosensitive gelatin-PNIPAAm (GN) hydrogels for the topical treatment of intraocular diseases such as glaucoma have not yet been reported, probably because of their insufficient ability to facilitate the permeation of eye drops through the corneal barriers.

An effective approach for improving the therapeutic outcome of a pharmaceutical formulation is to improve its drug bioavailability on the biological entities of interest [19,20]. In ophthalmology, tears constitute the first ocular barrier to pharmaceutical formulations, and their mucous layer is the first platform for the formulations to be stably anchored. Therefore, endowing an ophthalmic formulation with mucoadhesive characteristics can significantly increase the drug retention time in the pre-corneal region, thereby enhancing drug permeation through the underlying corneal epithelium. In this regard, the introduction of thiol groups into the backbone chains of polymers is expected to enhance the mucoadhesive properties via the formation of disulfide bonds between the thiolated polymers and cysteine-rich subdomains of the mucus glycoproteins [21–23]. Among the potential thiol-containing candidates, glutathione (GSH) is a suitable option because it is a natural component present in living organisms and mammal tears [24–26]. Remarkably, the incorporation of GSH can significantly enhance the permeation effects of the resultant thiolated polymers [27,28]. These effects can be elucidated by the result of the inhibition/inactivation of the protein tyrosine phosphatase via the thiol/disulfide exchange reaction, which induces tight-junction opening [28,29]. Inspired by these findings, it was hypothesized that functionalizing GN hydrogels with GSH molecules can significantly improve the bioavailability of eye drops in pre-corneal regions by exploiting the mucoadhesive properties and permeation effects of the thiolated polymer hydrogels.

Gelatin (type A, 300 Bloom); 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC); pilocarpine nitrate; 2,2′-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH); hydrogen peroxide; matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9, EC 3.4.24.35); α-chymotrypsin; and GSH were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAm) were supplied by Acros Organics (Geel, Belgium). The NIPAAm was further purified via recrystallization in n-hexane. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) was acquired from Biochrom (Berlin, Germany). Eagle’s Minimum Essential Medium (MEM) was purchased from Gibco-BRL (Grand Island, NY, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Biological Industries (Kibbutz Beit Haemek, Israel). All the other chemicals were of reagent grade and were used as received, without any purification.

To synthesize the multifunctional hydrogels, aminated gelatin and carboxylic end-capped PNIPAAm were first prepared as described elsewhere [30]. Subsequently, 10 g of PNIPAAm-COOH was dissolved in 50 mL of a 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES) buffer containing 2.59 g of EDC and 1.55 g of NHS, and the resultant mixture was agitated for 6 h. Next, 50 mL of the MES buffer containing 1 g of the aminated gelatin was added to the agitated mixture, and the reaction was allowed to proceed for 24 h at 25 °C. The resultant product was then precipitated at 50 °C, centrifuged, and re-suspended in deionized water at room temperature (25 °C). The dispersion was exhaustively dialyzed against deionized water at 4 °C for 4 d to remove residues. The dialyzed dispersion was lyophilized at –50 °C and stored in an enclosed vessel under ambient conditions for later use as the GN material.

Functionalization of the GN material with GSH was achieved via a free-radical grafting method. Briefly, 1 g of the GN material was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. Next, 0.25 g of ascorbic acid and 1 mL of hydrogen peroxide were added to the resultant aqueous solution, and the mixture was kept at 25 °C for 2 h. Subsequently, different amounts of GSH were added (60, 120, and 240 mg, referred to as GSH1, GSH2, and GSH3, respectively), and the reaction mixture was agitated for 24 h. The mixture was then exhaustively dialyzed against deionized water for 4 d at 4 °C to remove unreacted components. Finally, the dialyzed product was lyophilized at –50 °C and stored in an enclosed vessel under ambient conditions for subsequent use as GN-GSH hydrogels.

The presence of functional groups and characteristic peaks of GN and GN-GSH samples were identified using proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The 1H NMR spectra were recorded at 500 MHz using a Bruker AVANCE DRX 500 NMR instrument. The FTIR spectra were measured in the range of 3700–800 cm−1 with a resolution of 8 cm−1 by using a FT-730 ATR/FTIR spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan). The thiol-group content of the GSH-functionalized GN material was determined via Ellman’s test (n = 4). The water content of the GN and GN-GSH samples was measured by dissolving the polymeric samples in 1.5 mL of deionized water to obtain solutions (10% w/v) at 25 °C, followed by a gelling process at 32 °C for 10 min. The as-obtained hydrogels were then vacuum-dried until they reached a constant weight, which is referred to as the initial weight (W i) of the samples. Swollen samples (weight of W s) were obtained by immersing the dehydrated samples in an artificial tear solution (ATS) containing 124 mM Na+, 133 mM Cl−, 24 mM HCO3−, 30 mM K+, 0.7 mM Mg2+, 0.7 mM Ca2+, 0.35 mM glucose, 4.5 mM urea, 3.5 mM acetate, and 0.2 mM pyruvate at 32 °C for 4 h under shaking conditions. The water content (%) of the samples was calculated as ((W s – W i)/W s) × 100 (n = 4). The lower critical solution temperature (LCST) of the GN and GN-GSH samples was determined using differential scanning calorimetry (DSC, TA Instruments, New Castle, DE, USA). For sample preparation, the polymeric material was dissolved in ATS at a concentration of 10% w/v. Subsequently, 8 mg of the solution was hermetically sealed in an Al pan and conditioned at room temperature for 1 h. DSC measurements were performed by heating the sample from 23 to 37 °C with a ramp rate of 3 °C/min under a dry N2 atmosphere. The LCST was defined as the onset of the endothermic peak in the DSC spectrum (n = 4). For weight-loss measurement, the gelation of the GN and GN-GSH samples was performed in a 32 °C water bath for 10 min using 10% (w/v) sample solutions. The initial dry weight (W i) of the test materials was recorded after a stable weight was reached under vacuum conditions. Subsequently, the GN and GN-GSH samples were immersed in ATS containing 160 ng/mL of MMP-9 at 32 °C. The degradation medium was replaced with a fresh buffer having the same MMP-9 concentration at predetermined time points. At each time point, the degraded samples were gathered and washed with deionized water at 32 °C. Subsequently, the samples were dried under vacuum conditions, and the dry weight (W d) was measured. The weight-loss percentage (%) was determined as follows: ((W i – W d)/W i) × 100 (n = 5).

Statens Seruminstitut Rabbit Cornea (SIRC) cells (BCRC no. 60093) were purchased from the Bioresource Collection and Research Center (Hsinchu, Taiwan, ROC). These cells were grown in a culture medium (MEM), which was supplemented with 10% FBS and 2% non-essential amino acids. The cultures were incubated under a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 at 37 °C. The medium was replaced twice per week. The cells were subcultured via trypsinization at a split ratio of 1:3. SIRC cells were seeded into 24-well plates (1 mL/well) at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well. Each well of a 24-well plate was divided into two compartments using cell-culture inserts (Falcon 3095, Becton Dickinson Labware, Franklin Lakes, NJ, USA). The test samples prepared via the temperature-triggered in situ gel method (as previously mentioned) were added to the inner well of the double-chamber system. Cells grown without the polymeric material served as a control (Ctrl) group. A phase-contrast microscope (Nikon, Melville, NY, USA) was employed to observe the morphology of the SIRC cells after the cell cultures were exposed to different types of polymeric materials for 2 d. Moreover, a cell-proliferation MTS assay was performed to estimate the metabolic activity. The absorbance at 490 nm was measured using a microplate spectrophotometer. Data were expressed as the MTS activity relative to that of the Ctrl (n = 6). To assess the genotoxicity of SIRC cells incubated with the GN and GN-GSH samples, an alkaline comet assay was performed. After cell lysis, the slides were exposed to the optimum electrophoresis conditions and stained with DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) for imaging using a fluorescence microscope (Axiovert 200 M, Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). The DNA damage for each cell was quantified as follows: comet tail length (μm) = maximum total length – head diameter. For the calculations, 200 cells (50 cells from each of the quadruplicate slides) were arbitrarily selected, and the tail lengths were averaged (n = 6).

To study the DPPH free-radical scavenging properties, 250 mg of the test hydrogel was dissolved in 12.5 mL of deionized water, followed by mixing with an equal volume of an ethanol solution that contained 1 mg of DPPH radicals. The resultant mixture was incubated at 25 °C for 30 min. The absorbance at 517 nm was measured using an ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The DPPH scavenging activity (%) was calculated using the following equation: ((A 0 – A 1)/A 0) × 100, where A 0 represents the absorbance of the DPPH solution in the absence of the test hydrogel and A 1 represents the absorbance of the DPPH solution (prepared under the same reaction conditions) incubated with the hydrogel (n = 5).

To evaluate the antioxidant activity of the hydrogels against oxidative stress, the SIRC cells were maintained in a regular growth medium containing MEM, 10% FBS, and 1% antibiotic/antimycotic solution. Before exposure to hydrogen peroxide, the SIRC cells (density of 5 × 104 cells/well) were seeded in 24-well plates and incubated with 150 μL of the hydrogel solution (1 mg/mL) for 24 h. The resultant cell cultures were then incubated for 24 h in a medium containing hydrogen peroxide (200 μM). Cells exposed to 0 (Ctrl group) or 200 (HP group) μM hydrogen peroxide for 24 h (without 24 h of pretreatment with any hydrogel) (1 mg/mL) were used for comparison. The intracellular production of ROS was measured by staining the cells with 10 μM 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) (Molecular Probes, Eugene, OR, USA) at 37 °C for 1 h. Fluorescence images (Ex. 488 nm; Em. 525 nm) were obtained using a fluorescence microscope (Axiovert 200 M; Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). The fluorescence reading was obtained via a multimode microplate reader (BioTek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA) to determine the difference in the fluorescence intensity (n = 4).

The transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) in a cell-culture model of the epithelial monolayer was measured to assess the possibility of using hydrogel materials for opening cellular tight junctions. SIRC layers were grown on polycarbonate filters, which were coated with 1.3 mg/mL of rat tail collagen type I (Becton Dickinson Labware, Franklin Lakes, NJ, USA). The cells were maintained in a regular growth medium at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 for 10 d to reach confluence. The cells were then moved to an air-interface culture condition, and the medium was replaced once a day during the cultivation for 3 weeks [31]. Subsequently, the test materials were added to the apical chamber, where the cell culture was located. The TEER was measured using an EVOM resistance meter (World Precision Instruments, Sarasota, FL, USA). The resistance of the polycarbonate membrane in the culture filters was subtracted from all readings. All experiments were performed in quadruplicate, and the TEER change that indicated the tight-junction disruption was expressed as the percentage of the TEER relative to the initial value (0 h). The bioadhesion between the hydrogels and rabbit corneal tissues was evaluated using a texture analyzer (TA-XT.Plus, Stable Micro Systems, UK) with a 50-N load cell. The tissue was bonded to the cylinder probe surface via double-sided adhesive tape, and the hydrogel sample was stabilized on a holder stage at 32 ± 1 °C. Consequently, the cylinder probe was moved downward with a speed of 1 mm/s and a trigger force of 0.049 N, contacted the hydrogel for 10 s, and was finally withdrawn at a speed of 0.5 mm/s, with the same force. The detachment force (maximum force needed to separate the tissue from the hydrogel) was determined using the Texture Exponent 32 software (n = 4). The drug-release profiles of the hydrogel were measured by preparing the polymeric solution (10% w/v) in ATS, followed by mixing with pilocarpine nitrate (2% w/v) at 25 °C. After gelation at 32 °C, the drug-containing gelling material was transferred to ATS in the presence of 160 ng of MMP-9. After incubation at 32 °C under agitation conditions for predetermined time periods, the release buffer was collected and analyzed via high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), with a UV detector absorbing at 216 nm. The pilocarpine concentrations released from the hydrogel samples were determined using a calibration curve (n = 5). The cumulative release percentage of the drug at the predetermined time point was calculated by dividing the amount of released pilocarpine by the total pilocarpine amount and multiplying by 100.

All the procedures for the animal studies were approved by the Institutional Review Board of Chang Gung University and were conducted in accordance with the ARVO Statement for the Use of Animals in Ophthalmic and Vision Research. Adult New Zealand white rabbits (National Laboratory Animal Breeding and Research Center, Taipei, Taiwan, ROC) with weight of 3.0–3.5 kg and age of 16–20 weeks were used. Twenty-four healthy rabbits were equally divided into four groups (GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3) for in vivo retention studies. For each group, 50 μL of a carrier material solution (10% w/v) was instilled onto the lower conjunctival sacs of the left eyes. The lower conjunctival sacs of the right eyes were left untreated, for comparison of the ocular conditions. The resistance of the test hydrogels to the clearance action of the eye was verified by pulling down the lower eyelids and performing imaging using slit-lamp biomicroscopy. Thus, the mucoadhesive performance of the samples was assessed. After 1, 7, and 14 d of instillation on the rabbit eyes, the residual hydrogel materials were collected by washing the ocular surface with a cold buffer solution. The retention of the hydrogel carrier was evaluated by analyzing the amount of hydroxyproline [32]. Briefly, the samples were mixed with a chloramine-T reagent, followed by 25 min of oxidation at room temperature. Then, Ehrlich’s aldehyde reagent was added to the oxidized sample for absorbance measurement at 550 nm (ThermoLabsystems, Vantaa, Finland). The amounts of hydroxyproline in the GN and GN-GSH hydrogels were quantified via comparison with standard calibration curves. The percentage of hydrogel carrier retention was determined by dividing the amount of hydroxyproline in the collected sample by the initial total amount of hydroxyproline in the hydrogel applied to the ocular surface and multiplying by 100 (n = 6).

Adult New Zealand white rabbits subjected to experimental glaucoma were used to evaluate the pharmacological treatment efficacy of the hydrogels loaded with pilocarpine. For 30 rabbits, glaucoma was induced via α-chymotrypsin (0.1 mg/mL) injection into the posterior chamber of the rabbit eyes. The animals were divided into four test groups (GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3), with six rabbits per group, and the glaucomatous rabbits received topical instillation of 50 μL of a mixture containing pilocarpine (2% w/v) and carrier material solution (10% w/v). The remaining six rabbits afflicted with experimental glaucoma were not treated with any polymers or drugs (Ctrl groups). Investigations were performed during a follow-up period of 14 d. The IOP values were measured at predetermined time points by using a Schiotz tonometer (AMANN Ophthalmic Instruments, Liptingen, Germany). Five measurements were performed on one eye of each rabbit, and the average IOP value was utilized. The mean IOP values of healthy eyes at the preoperation stage were used as the baseline, and those of the tested eyes were expressed as the change to the baseline at each time point. Corneal topographic maps were recorded on day 14 after the pharmacological treatment using a Medmont E300 Corneal Topographer (Medmont Pty Ltd., Melbourne, Australia). Before the data acquisition, the corneal topographer was calibrated according to the manufacturer’s recommendations (±0.01 mm at four calibration surfaces). The mean keratometric (K) value, which indicated the average corneal curvature in the central 3 mm zone, was obtained from the topographic map. Six independent measurements were averaged for obtaining each data point. The cell morphology and density of the corneal endothelium of all the test rabbit eyes were characterized using a specular microscope (Topcon Optical, Tokyo, Japan). The endothelial cell density of each data point was obtained by averaging four independently measured values. To record electroretinography (ERG) spectra, the rabbits were anesthetized to eliminate electrical noise from the muscle activity. After the pupil dilation was maximized with a topical 1% cyclopentolate hydrochloride ophthalmic solution (Alcon), the rabbit eyes were tested. Dark adaptation was allowed for at least 30 min prior to application of an Ag/AgCl electrode (Biopac Systems, Santa Barbara, CA, USA) on the topically anesthetized cornea, a reference electrode on the ear, and a subcutaneous ground electrode on the neck of the animal. The electrophysiological responses were averaged for each run, and the mean value for five independent runs was used for the subsequent analysis of the peak amplitudes of the a- and b-waves of the ERG. The a-wave was measured as the difference in amplitude between the recording at the onset and the trough of the negative deflection, and the b-wave was measured as the difference in amplitude between the trough of the a-wave and the peak of the b-wave. Each data point was an average of six independent measurements.

To measure the concentrations of pilocarpine released from the drug-loaded hydrogels into the anterior chamber, the aqueous humor in each test rabbit eye was aspirated by a 30-gauge needle and then analyzed using HPLC. In other experiments, aqueous-humor specimens were collected for biochemical analysis. The total antioxidant level was determined using a method described elsewhere [33]. An assay was performed to evaluate the ability of the biological fluid to inhibit the thiobarbituric-acid reactive substances, which were produced by sodium benzoate under the influence of the free oxygen radicals derived from Fenton’s reaction. Calibration curves (in the range of 0–2 mM) were created with uric acid. Each data point represented the average of four independent measurements. The nitrite level in the aqueous humor was determined using the Griess reaction, in accordance with a previous report [34]. Calibration curves were created with sodium nitrite in the range of 0–100 µM. Each data point represented the average of four independent measurements. The presented data for the total antioxidant and nitrite levels were both averaged for six animals in each group.

For the final experiments (i.e., 14 d after single-drop administration), the rabbits were euthanized with CO2 gas to harvest ocular tissues. After being permeabilized with acetone and washed with PBS containing 1% Triton X-100 and 1% dimethyl sulfoxide, the corneal tissues were incubated in 1% bovine serum albumin diluted in the buffer for 1 h to block nonspecific binding. The corneal tissues were respectively processed for immunofluorescence staining with occludin and a nuclear fluorescence dye (DAPI), as reported previously [35]. The mean fluorescence intensities of the stains were analyzed using the software ImageJ (n = 6). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed to examine the morphological structures of the myelin of the optic-nerve cross sections for the test rabbit eyes. Optic-nerve segments were dissected and double-fixed at 4 °C in a 2.5% paraformaldehyde solution and 0.5% osmium tetroxide in PBS, embedded in Araldite-Epon, and infiltrated with 5% uranyl acetate and lead citrate [36]. Ultrathin sections were placed on Cu grids for TEM observations; the axon density was calculated as the mean value across random micrographs (n = 6) [37].
Results were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). The significance was evaluated via a one-way analysis of variance (P < 0.05).

Fig. 1 schematically illustrates the formulation of the eye drops containing GN-GSH hydrogels and their functions in glaucoma therapy. Specifically, the synthesized GN-GSH hydrogels are employed as follows: (I) the hydrogel carrier is loaded with pilocarpine (an anti-glaucoma agent) to produce a new eye-drop formulation; (II) the as-formulated eye drops are instilled onto the conjunctival sac of the glaucomatous eye to alleviate the disease-related oxidative damage through antioxidative and cytoprotective actions; (III) in situ gelling of the eye-drop solution occurs upon contact with the biological tissue at the physiological temperature; (IV) disulfide bonds are formed between the GN-GSH gelling formulation and cysteine-rich subdomains of mucus glycoproteins (resulting in strong adhesion between the gelling formulation and the mucous layer of the tear film and thereby extending the residence time of the gelling formulation on the pre-corneal region); and (V) the permeation effects of the gelling formulation are enhanced via occludin proteolysis, which is caused by the inhibition of the protein tyrosine phosphatase. Thus, the GSH can enhance the mucoadhesive characteristics of the hydrogel carrier and mediate the permeation effects on the drug transport across the corneal barrier, consequently improving the retention of the polymer hydrogel in the pre-corneal region and assisting the pilocarpine molecules to access the anterior chamber for enhancing the drug bioavailability and treatment efficacy.

To obtain an optimized ophthalmic formulation, different GSH feeding amounts were used to functionalize the GN hydrogel. First, 1H NMR spectroscopy was employed to determine the presence of GSH on the backbone chains of the GN samples. The chemical shift scale was referenced against internal DMSO‑d 6 at 2.50 ppm. Fig. 2 a shows the 1H NMR spectra of the different GN-GSH hydrogels, with the chemical shift assignments for different protons (1.1, 1.6, and 2.1 ppm) of the GN hydrogel [38,39]. A comparison of the 1H NMR spectrum of the GN hydrogel provided proof of the incorporation of GSH in all the GN-GSH samples, as signified by the appearance and prominence of a peak in the range of 3.0–4.0 ppm. These characteristic chemical shifts have been reported for GSH cysteine Hβ, glutamic acid Hα, and glycine Hα [40]. Interestingly, as the GSH feeding amount was increased (from GN-GSH1 to GN-GSH3), the intensity of the characteristic signal increased, indicating the presence of larger amounts of GSH in the GN hydrogels. Additionally, FTIR measurements were performed to detect the functional groups. As shown in Fig. 2b, the FTIR spectrum of the GN hydrogel sample had characteristic peaks corresponding to N–H bending at 1539 cm−1, CO stretching vibration at 1641 cm−1, and the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H at 3265 cm−1 [30,41]. Similar FTIR profiles were obtained for all the GN-GSH samples, with the exception of a new peak at 2568 cm−1. This peak is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of S-H, implying the formation of a covalent bond between the functional GSH and the GN hydrogels [42,43]. As the feeding amount of GSH was increased, the intensity of the peak at 2568 cm−1 increased. To verify these qualitative 1H NMR and FTIR results, the amounts of free thiol groups in the GN-GSH hydrogels were evaluated using Ellman’s reagent (Fig. S1). The concentrations of free thiol groups in GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3 were 60 ± 15 μmol/g polymer, 156 ± 28 μmol/g polymer, and 380 ± 42 μmol/g polymer, respectively. These data confirm that for a given GN hydrogel, the use of a larger feeding amount of GSH resulted in a larger number of free thiol groups in the carrier after the biomaterial functionalization. Additionally, excessive GSH feeding amounts resulted in a similar grafting level to that of the GN-GSH3 sample (data not shown), probably owing to the amount of amino groups in the GN hydrogel, which was saturated with respect to the GSH3 feeding amount.

The hydrophilic characteristics of the different GN-GSH hydrogels, which can affect their biocompatibility with tears and their area of contact with the cornea, were assessed by measuring their water contents. Fig. 3 a shows the water contents of the GN hydrogel and its GSH-functionalized counterparts. The GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3 samples had equilibrium water contents of 56.8% ± 1.2%, 61.6% ± 1.5%, 65.8% ± 1.1%, and 70.4% ± 0.9%, respectively. The water content increased with the amount of GSH in the hydrogel, probably owing to the increase in the amount of hydrophilic GSH molecules [44]. A larger amount of GSH led to larger spaces between the hydrogel macromolecules, which allowed more water to access the gelling matrix. Furthermore, the low critical solution temperature (LCST) values estimated from the DSC thermograms (Fig. 3b) for GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3 were 26.4 ± 0.2 °C, 27.0 ± 0.2 °C, 28.6 ± 0.3 °C, and 30.1 ± 0.4 °C, respectively. Thus, the LCST increased with the number of hydrophilic molecules. These molecules improved the water-absorption capability, which increased the phase transition temperature and suppressed the gelation process [45]. This general trend of the LCST indicates that a larger amount of GSH (i.e., containing more hydrophilic molecules) can induce a higher LCST. The effect of the amount of GSH on the biodegradation of the hydrogel was examined at 32 °C in a physiological medium containing 160 ng/mL MMP-9. During the follow-up period of 14 d, the weight losses of the GN and GN-GSH hydrogels gradually increased over time (Fig. 3c); however, the degradation was different at each time point. The weight losses for the GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3 samples were 32.3% ± 1.2%, 34.7% ± 0.8%, 40.0% ± 1.5%, and 45.8% ± 1.1%, respectively, on day 1, suggesting the key role of the GSH functionalization level. On day 14, all the samples were significantly degraded compared with day 1, as revealed by the weight-loss percentages, which were in the range of 50%–70%. The variations in the weight losses of the tested hydrogels are ascribed to their different water-wetting behaviors, which were modulated by the amount of GSH in the hydrogel. A larger amount of GSH endowed the carrier material with higher hydrophilicity, allowing the proteinase to access the gelling matrix at a higher rate [46], i.e., faster degradation.

The in vitro biocompatibility of the GN-GSH hydrogels was examined using SIRC cultures to evaluate the potential of these hydrogels as ophthalmic carrier materials for eye-drop solutions. Phase-contrast micrographs (Fig. S2a) of SIRC cells incubated with different types of hydrogels (test groups) and no material (Ctrl group) indicated that none of the GN-GSH samples affected the characteristic morphology of the cells; i.e., the test materials exhibited excellent biocompatibility with the ocular cells. To support this microscopic observation, the SIRC cell growth was assessed via an MTS assay (Fig. S2b). After 2 d of exposure to the hydrogels, the metabolic activities for the Ctrl, GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3 samples were 100.0% ± 4.5%, 98.0% ± 2.9%, 96.3% ± 4.1%, 98.0% ± 3.0%, and 95.1% ± 3.8%, respectively. The comparable activity levels for all the cell cultures confirmed the high biocompatibility of all the test GN-GSH hydrogels with the ocular cells. Additionally, the possibility of the GN-GSH hydrogels causing genotoxicity was evaluated via an alkaline comet assay. Fluorescence-microscopy images (Fig. S2c) of the SIRC cells from all the groups showed intact nuclei with negligible comet tails, indicating that the GN-GSH hydrogels are harmless and do not cause significant damage to DNA. Quantitative results for the comet-like tail lengths of all the samples are shown in Fig. S2d. The tail lengths for the Ctrl, GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3 samples were 1.0 ± 0.7 µm, 1.7 ± 0.5 µm, 1.6 ± 0.7 µm, 1.2 ± 0.8 µm, and 1.1 ± 1.0 µm, respectively. The results confirm the negligible genotoxicity caused by the GN-GSH hydrogels. Collectively, the in vitro studies proved that the hydrogel materials were highly biocompatible, suggesting their potential for eye-drop formulations.

Because GSH is an antioxidant and oxidative stress is a major pathological factor in glaucoma [24,47], it was important to determine whether the GN-GSH hydrogels had antioxidant activity, which is highly beneficial for alleviating glaucoma-associated oxidative damage. Thus, the effect of the amount of GSH in the hydrogel on the free-radical-scavenging capabilities was examined using the DPPH method. Because the free radicals were quenched by the antioxidant, the color of the test DPPH solution changed from purple to yellow [48]. Fig. 4 a shows typical photographs of DPPH reagent solutions that reacted with the GN and GN-GSH hydrogels and no substance (blank). There was a gradual color transformation from dark purple (blank sample) to light purple (GN and GN-GSH1 samples) and then to yellow (GN-GSH2 and GN-GSH3 samples), indicating the different antioxidant levels of the hydrogels for the reduction of purple chromogen radicals. Quantitatively, the DPPH radical inhibitions for GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3 were 19.1% ± 2.8%, 31.5% ± 3.1%, 72.0% ± 2.9%, and 91.9% ± 4.0%, respectively (Fig. S3a), suggesting that a larger amount of GSH can endow the hydrogel carrier with a stronger DPPH-scavenging capability. This increase in DPPH inhibition is ascribed to the contribution of more antioxidant GSH molecules [49]. Because intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) production can trigger reactions with most cellular components and thus induce DNA damage, protein denaturation, lipid peroxidation, or even cell death [50], the antioxidant capabilities of the GN-GSH hydrogels for suppressing intracellular ROS production were investigated. Fig. 4b shows fluorescence-microscopy images of hydrogen peroxide-treated SIRC cultures incubated with the different test hydrogels (GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3). The healthy cell cultures (Ctrl) and hydrogen peroxide-induced cultures (HP) without hydrogel incubation are included for comparison. The green fluorescence signals in the microscopy images qualitatively indicate the levels of intracellular ROS production. These signals suggest that the Ctrl group had minimal ROS production, whereas the maximum production was observed for the HP groups. The test hydrogels had different green fluorescence signals, indicating the effect of the amount of GSH on the antioxidant activities of the hydrogels. As the GSH amount was increased, the ROS production was enhanced, possibly because of the larger amount of antioxidant (GSH). Quantitative analyses of the fluorescence signals for all the groups supported the qualitative observations, yielding the following ranking for the intracellular ROS attenuation: GN-GSH3 > GN-GSH2 > GN-GSH1 > GN.

Additionally, GSH has been reported to be capable of opening tight junctions by inhibiting the protein tyrosine phosphatase and hence the proteolysis of occludin [28,29]. Therefore, the TEER of the corneal epithelial layers in a cell-culture model was measured to investigate the possibility of using GN-GSH to alter the integrity of tight junctions in response to the GSH functionalization level. The reduction of the TEER value (percentage relative to the Ctrl group) gradually increased with the amount of GSH (Fig. 4c), implying the important role of the amount of GSH in loosening the corneal epithelial tightness. Thus, a larger amount of GSH endowed the GN hydrogel with a greater ability to open tight junctions, as signified by the larger TEER reduction, thereby allowing the transportation of more ions along the paracellular route. To assess the mucoadhesive properties of the GN-GSH hydrogels, their adhesion forces were assessed ex vivo on rabbit corneas. As expected, the addition of GSH to the GN backbone chains improved the mucoadhesion of the hydrogel, and the enhancement of the bioadhesive characteristic was proportional to the amount of GSH (Fig. 4d). Furthermore, in vitro drug-release studies were performed to evaluate the feasibility of using these hydrogels as ophthalmic carriers. Pilocarpine loading did not result in significant changes in the LCST values of the GN and GN-GSH hydrogels (Fig. S4). The GN and GN-GSH hydrogels exhibited different cumulative pilocarpine release profiles during the 14-d follow-up period Fig. 5 ). Most of the drug entrapped by the GN-GSH3 hydrogel was released (~95%) on day 5, and the sustained release profiles were extended to 12 and 14 d for the GN-GSH2 and GN-GSH1 samples, respectively. This trend of degradation of the sustained release performance with an increase in the amount of GSH is attributed to the enhanced water-wetting properties. These properties facilitated water diffusion into the gelling matrix, which elevated the hydrogels to highly swollen regimes (in proportion to the amount of GSH) and thereby created more spaces through which drug molecules could diffuse out of the matrix. Collectively, it can be concluded that the amount of GSH played an important role in the hydrogel, imparting antioxidant activities, mucoadhesive characteristics, and hydrophilic properties to the GN-GSH samples. Increasing the amount of GSH in the GN hydrogel enhanced the therapeutic activity but degraded the drug-release performance of the ophthalmic carrier.

To further evaluate the applicability of the GN-GSH hydrogels as mucoadhesive drug carriers, the ocular retention and mucoadhesion ability of the hydrogels were examined in vivo via slit-lamp biomicroscopic observations. Various aqueous formulations (GN and GN-GSH solutions) were topically instilled into the lower conjunctival sacs of rabbit eyes. Once the aqueous solutions contacted the sac, they were quickly converted into gelling forms (observed as white lumps in Fig. 6 a) because of the thermally induced gelling properties of the test hydrogels. The gelling materials with smaller amounts of GSH were perceived to be more solid, probably because of the lower LCST. The wettability of the gelling material on the ocular surface depended on the amount of GSH, with a smaller amount resulting in a smaller contact area between the two entities. A trend similar to that of day 1 was observed for the physical appearances of all the gelling materials on the cul-de-sacs of the rabbit eyes on day 7 of the follow-up period, except for the reduced size/amount of the white lumps (i.e., gelling formulation). Nevertheless, the gelling materials had different appearances on day 14. No gelling material was observed in the rabbit eyes topically treated with the GN sample, whereas the gel was retained on the ocular surfaces instilled with GN-GSH counterparts. Among all the test groups, GN-GSH2 exhibited the largest amount of remaining gelling formulation. These results are ascribed to the different resistances of the gelling materials to the clearance action of the eye. Because the GN hydrogel had the poorest mucoadhesive property, its gel was washed out completely on day 14. Adding a small amount of GSH to the GN hydrogel (i.e., GN-GSH1) improved the mucoadhesion. Thus, the maintenance of the gelling material could be extended to a certain degree. Although the GN-GSH3 hydrogel had the best mucoadhesive performance on the cornea, it also exhibited the highest degradability, resulting in a significant loss of gelling material. By achieving a tradeoff between the mucoadhesion and degradation factors, the GN-GSH2 material can extend the existence of the gel on the ocular surface for a period of > 14 d. To support these qualitative observations, tears and gel residues from all the test rabbit eyes were gathered and analyzed. Fig. 6b shows the quantitative results for the carrier-material retention, which was defined as the percentage of the initial amount of applied carrier material. The retentions for GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3 were 58.1% ± 5.8%, 63.8% ± 4.2%, 65.9% ± 2.7%, and 38.0% ± 2.2% at day 1, respectively. These material retentions were different on day 14 of the follow-up period, and the highest retention of the carrier material (22.0% ± 0.6%) was observed for the GN-GSH2 sample. Importantly, the instillation of the different GN-GSH hydrogels on the cul-de-sacs did not cause any ocular discomfort or irritation in the rabbit eyes, implying that the GN-GSH hydrogels have sufficient biocompatibility to be employed as carriers for ophthalmic drugs.

For pharmacological-treatment studies, α-chymotrypsin was used to experimentally induce glaucoma in rabbit eyes, which were then utilized to evaluate the use of the aqueous GN-GSH formulations (containing pilocarpine) as eye drops for the treatment of glaucoma. Clinically, IOP measurements were first performed on the glaucomatous eyes after the instillation of the aqueous ophthalmic formulations, and glaucomatous eyes left without pharmacological treatment were used as a Ctrl group for comparison. The obtained IOP values for all the test rabbit eyes were recorded and are shown in Fig. 7 , where the dashed baseline shows the IOP for healthy eyes at the pre-operation stage. As-induced glaucomatous (GL) eyes exhibited increased IOP values of 22.5 ± 1.3 mmHg, and these high values varied differently in response to the topical administration of the aqueous GN and GN-GSH formulations. The IOP values for the Ctrl group remained high (with slight increments) throughout the follow-up period of 14 d, validating the experimental model of glaucoma. Then, 12 h after a single topical instillation, the IOP values for all the glaucomatous eyes were reduced to levels close to the baseline. The formulations with larger amounts of GSH reduced the IOP more efficiently in first 3 d of the follow-up period. On day 7, the IOP values for the GN and GN-GSH1 groups had reverted to 20.9 ± 1.9 mmHg and 12.8 ± 1.0 mmHg, respectively, whereas those for GN-GSH2 and GN-GSH3 remained low (close to the baseline), indicating the significant impact of the amount of GSH incorporated onto the backbone chains in the GN hydrogel. These effects became more obvious over time (e.g., on days 10 and 14); only the GN-GSH2 formulation kept the IOP values for the glaucomatous eyes close to the baseline. The obtained IOP profiles confirmed the significant role of the amount of GSH in regulating the IOP values of glaucomatous eyes, suggesting that a low GSH feeding amount (60 mg GSH added to 1 g of GN) is insufficient for triggering the antioxidant activity of the GSH, whereas excess GSH feeding amount (240 mg GSH added to 1 g of GN) can cause the rapid degradation of the gelling formulation (i.e., making it easier to wash out through the clearance action of the eye).

In clinical ophthalmology, corneal topography is a noninvasive and powerful method for diagnosing and controlling various ocular diseases [51]. Changes in the surface curvature of the cornea (and thus the structure of the eye) in response to disease development/treatment can be assessed by mapping the cornea. Fig. 8 a shows topographic maps recorded for the test rabbit eyes, which included healthy (Pre), glaucomatous (GL), untreated glaucomatous (Ctrl), and treated glaucomatous (GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3) eyes, on day 14 of the follow-up period. The nearly uniform distribution of the green color for the healthy corneas (Pre) indicates low levels of corneal aberration. In contrast, the non-uniform color map with mixed colors (green, yellow, and red) for the as-induced glaucomatous corneas (GL) indicates high aberration levels. The prominent manifestation of only red signals for the Ctrl, GN, GN-GSH1, and GN-GSH3 groups suggests the existence of curved corneas with high refractive powers. These results are ascribed to the active/effective time of the test ophthalmic formulations, which could not alleviate the glaucoma progression for a lengthy period. In contrast, the single-dose therapy using the aqueous GN-GSH2 formulation was able to repair the glaucomatous corneas over a long period of 14 d, as signified by the low aberration (i.e., uniform green distribution compared with that of the Pre group). To support these topographic maps, the mean keratometric K values for all the animal eyes were calculated, as shown in Fig. 8b. The K values for the Pre, GL, and Ctrl groups were 41.3 ± 1.0 D, 46.7 ± 0.9 D, and 50.8 ± 0.8 D, respectively, clearly indicating the progression of the disease. The GN-GSH2 group had the lowest K value (42.2 ± 0.7 D) among all the test eye-drop formulations, confirming that it had the highest treatment efficacy.

A high IOP and oxidative stress are the main risk factors that can strongly affect the structure and integrity of the corneal endothelium, which is a thin cellular monolayer that maintains corneal deturgescence and transparency [52,53]. Therefore, the treatment efficacy of the different aqueous formulations was assessed via the response of the corneal endothelium to the topical administration (Fig. 8c). For the Pre group, a characteristic pattern consisting of hexagonal cells was clearly observed. For the GL and Ctrl groups, the endothelial layers gradually lost their characteristic morphology, as revealed by enlarged and irregular cellular shapes (a sign of glaucoma progression). The cellular morphological observations for the GN, GN-GSH1, and GN-GSH3 groups were similar to those for the Ctrl group, possibly owing to the insufficient treatment efficacy of these formulations over the long follow-up period. However, the GN-GSH2 and GL groups had comparable cellular morphologies, indicating that the incorporation of a moderate amount of GSH into the GN hydrogels helped to repair the irregular cells and thus prevent glaucoma progression. The quantitative results (Fig. 8d) for the corneal endothelial cell densities of the different test groups were analyzed using specular microscopy. As the disease was initiated and progressed for the follow-up period of 14 d, the cell density in the test animal eyes gradually decreased from 3320 ± 101 cells/mm2 (Pre) to 3012 ± 105 cells/mm2 (GL) and then to 2518 ± 121 cells/mm2 (Ctrl). Treatments using the GN, GN-GSH1, and GN-GSH3 formulations did not assist in preventing cell degeneration, as indicated by densities equivalent to those of the Ctrl group. The cell density for the GN-GSH2 group was 3051 ± 108 cells/mm2, which was comparable to that for the GL group, suggesting that the aqueous GN-GSH2 formulation had a long period of activity and constitutes an efficient treatment to prevent glaucoma progression. These results indicate the significance of the amount of GSH in the GN hydrogel for effectively preventing corneal endothelial cell degeneration (and hence glaucoma progression). A moderate amount of GSH can provide a tradeoff between the sustained release performance and the therapeutic and mucoadhesive properties of the GN-GSH hydrogels.

A state of balance between the ROS and antioxidants is maintained under normal physiological conditions, and any reduction in antioxidant levels can lead to oxidative stress, which is a major risk factor in glaucoma. For instance, the antioxidant concentrations have been found to be exceedingly low in the aqueous humors of glaucomatous patients [54,55]. Additionally, elevated levels of nitric oxide have been associated with different pathological processes in glaucoma [56]. Thus, biochemical assays were performed to detect the antioxidant and nitrite levels in the aqueous humors of the test rabbit eyes. The total antioxidant levels (Fig. S5a) in healthy (Normal group) and untreated glaucomatous (Ctrl) eyes were determined to be 1.0 ± 0.09 and 0.19 ± 0.03 mM, respectively, suggesting that there was a decrease in the antioxidant activity for the aqueous humors of the glaucomatous eyes. The instillation of the GN and GN-GSH formulations assisted in preventing the reduction of the antioxidant activity, but with different efficacies. Among the groups of treated glaucomatous eyes, the GN-GSH2 formulation had the highest treatment efficacy, as indicated by the high total antioxidant level (0.73 ± 0.06 mM). No significant difference in treatment efficacy was observed between the GN and GN-GSH1 formulations. GN-GSH3, which had a high degradation rate, exhibited the lowest efficacy. The treatment efficacies of the GN and GN-GSH formulations were confirmed by the measured levels of nitrite in the aqueous humors of the treated glaucomatous eyes (Fig. S5b). The nitrite level for the GN-GSH2 group was the lowest among the test formulations, indicating the highest treatment effectiveness. The results indicate that the integration of an appropriate amount of GSH into the GN hydrogel can improve the antioxidant action in the aqueous humors of glaucomatous eyes, exhibiting considerable potential for targeting oxidative stress to alleviate the progression of glaucoma.

To demonstrate that the incorporation of GSH can significantly enhance the permeation effects owing to the capability of GSH to open cellular tight junctions [28,29], the corneal tissues of different eyes were subjected to immunofluorescence staining of occludin—a transmembrane protein that serves as a backbone of the tight junctions [57]. Fig. 9 a shows microscopic images of this immunofluorescence staining of the occludin in the corneal epithelium of the test rabbit eyes. The protein for the healthy (Pre) group exhibited thick and intact skeletons, suggesting the robust structure of the tight junctions in the corneal epithelium. This characteristic of continuous peripheral staining was maintained for the rabbit eyes afflicted with glaucoma (GL group), but thinner skeletons were observed. The GN-GSH2 group exhibited the highest degree of discontinuation/thinning in the patterned structure of the expressed protein in the epithelium, indicating that the appropriate amount of GSH induced the disruption of tight junctions (i.e., opening the tight junctions) to the largest extent for GN-GSH2. These results were supported by mean fluorescence intensity measurements (Fig. 9b), where the lowest intensity was obtained for the GN-GSH2 group (i.e., the largest loss of the stained transmembrane protein). Excessive or insufficient amounts of GSH were inappropriate for achieving effective tight-junction opening, possibly owing to the poor retention (GN-GSH3) or lack of active GSH (GN-GSH1 and GN) in the formulations instilled on the sac of the eye. As a proof of concept for the tight-junction-opening capability of the GN-GSH formulations that enhanced the permeation effects, the pilocarpine concentrations in the aqueous humors of the test rabbit eyes were quantified on the final day of the follow-up period. The pilocarpine concentrations (Fig. 9c) were 1.9 ± 0.6 µg/mL (GN), 3.4 ± 1.1 µg/mL (GN-GSH1), 15.8 ± 2.9 µg/mL (GN-GSH2), and 2.1 ± 0.5 µg/mL (GN-GSH3). The highest concentration was observed for GN-GSH2, confirming the hypothesis that the incorporation of a moderate amount of GSH into the GN hydrogel can significantly enhance the bioavailability by simultaneously providing the best mucoadhesive and tight-junction opening performance for the hydrogel. The corneal epithelial tight junctions can be recovered without significant structural damage after being opened by the GN-GSH hydrogels. This was confirmed by the gradual increase in the immunofluorescence signals of the occludin staining of the corneal epithelium on days 15 and 22 after the single topical administration following the removal of the hydrogel residues on day 14 post-administration (Fig. S6). The results suggest that the hydrogel is promising for long-term treatment of chronic glaucoma via decreasing the number of topical administrations. However, further systematic and more in-depth investigation is needed prior to clinical applications.

In the field of ophthalmology, ERG is a rapid, noninvasive tool for evaluating retinal function and is thus helpful for diagnosing glaucoma progression. It is noteworthy that the ERG patterns were strongly correlated with the IOP: the pattern waveform amplitudes decreased as the IOP increased [58]. Fig. 10 a shows the ERG patterns for the healthy (Pre), glaucomatous (GL), and treated glaucomatous (GN, GN-GSH1, GN-GSH2, and GN-GSH3) eyes 14 d after the topical administration. Similar waveform profiles were obtained for all the groups. Nevertheless, the amplitudes varied in response to the pharmacological treatments. As shown in Fig. 10b, the a- and b-wave amplitudes for the Pre group were 64.2 ± 3.1 and 266.0 ± 2.2 µV, respectively. The rabbit eyes afflicted with glaucoma (GL group) exhibited reduced amplitudes, indicating a deterioration of the electrophysiological functions of the retina in the diseased eyes. The reductions in the amplitudes for the glaucomatous eyes without treatment (Ctrl group) and those treated with the GN, GN-GSH1, and GN-GSH3 formulations implied insufficient treatment efficacies for preventing glaucoma progression. Interestingly, single topical instillation of the GN-GSH2 formulation on the lower conjunctival sac of the experimental glaucomatous eyes could alleviate disease progression, as signified by the waveform amplitudes comparable to those for the GL group.

Because oxidative stress in glaucomatous eyes can cause progressive degeneration of the optic nerve (i.e., the loss of and damage to retinal axons) and GSH is an antioxidant, cross sections of the optic nerves of the test rabbit eyes were analyzed using TEM to examine the changes in the axon morphology in response to the topical administration of the hydrogel formulations (Fig. 10c). A TEM image of a healthy optic nerve (Pre group) exhibited the characteristic morphology of retinal axons, which were densely compact, with uniform myelin sheaths. A significant alteration of the retinal axons was observed in the glaucomatous optic nerve (GL group), as revealed by the high degree of axon swelling, low axon density, and reduced thickness of the myelin sheaths, i.e., glaucoma-induced degeneration of the optic nerve [37]. The single-dose instillations of the GN and GN-GSH formulations yielded different behavior, with pharmacological regulation of the retinal axons in response to the amount of GSH. Compared with the GL group, the GN, GN-GSH1, and GN-GSH3 groups exhibited lower axon densities. Remarkably, a relatively condensed organization of the retinal axons with uniform myelin sheaths and a high axon density was obtained using the GN-GSH2 formulation. Quantitative results for the axon densities of the test optic nerves are presented in Fig. 10d. The axon density for the healthy optic nerves was 8.5 ± 0.7 × 104 axons/mm2. For the glaucomatous optic nerves, the axon density was reduced to 5.4 ± 0.3 × 104 axons/mm2 (GL group), suggesting axon degeneration in the glaucomatous eyes [37]. Pharmacological treatments using the GN, GN-GSH1, and GN-GSH3 formulations did not prevent the decrease in axon density compared with the Ctrl group. However, the axon density for the GN-GSH2 group 14 d after a single-dose instillation was comparable to that for the as-induced glaucomatous eyes (GL group), implying the pharmacological effectiveness of the GN-GSH2 formulation. This is ascribed to the prolonged ocular retention and enhanced paracellular delivery of the GN-GSH2 formulation, which improved the bioavailability of the anti-glaucoma (pilocarpine) and antioxidant (GSH) materials in the glaucomatous eyes, reducing the ocular hypertension, axon swelling, and myelin damage.

We developed a drug-delivery system with excellent mucoadhesive properties and permeation effects and investigated its applicability as multifunctional hydrogel eye drop for topical treatment of glaucoma. The drug-delivery system, which is composed of gelatin, PNIPAAm, and GSH, has good biocompatibility with the eye. Additionally, the therapeutic carrier material has tunable antioxidant properties, and a higher GSH functionalization level results in stronger free-radical scavenging capabilities. Interestingly, the incorporation of a moderate amount of GSH into the hydrogel carrier is highly effective for long-term retention on the conjunctival sac of rabbit eyes, possibly owing to a tradeoff between the bioadhesion and biodegradation properties. Pharmacological treatment based on single-dose topical instillation of GSH-functionalized hydrogels loaded with pilocarpine effectively prevented the disease progression in a glaucomatous rabbit for 14 d, while the use of a formulation without GSH exhibited a modest treatment efficacy, for only 3 d. We believe that the proposed multifunctional GSH-dependent drug-delivery system can be used to improve the bioavailability of various medications for efficient management of complex ocular diseases.
